title: 𒋳𒈠 𒀀𒉿𒈝
titleEn: Laws of Hammurabi — §11–25: theft II & the household (vii 62 – ix 65)
part: Laws §11–25 · theft II & the household (Col. vii 62 – ix 65)
draftNote: The § chips number the laws (Harper’s sectioning, still standard); long laws span several tap-units. Readings checked sign-by-sign against Harper’s 1904 edition.
source: Louvre stele, after R.F. Harper, The Code of Hammurabi, King of Babylon (Chicago, 1904): transliteration pp. 14–21, autograph Plates X–XV, List of Signs. Readings are Harper’s in modern subscript notation. Cuneiform in Old Babylonian monumental sign forms via the SantakkuM webfont by Sylvie Vanséveren (CC BY 4.0); pronunciation: conventional reconstructed Old Babylonian. Fresh translations made directly from the Akkadian; Harper’s public-domain translation consulted for sense only.
about: The lost-goods trial of §9–10 finishes its arc: the claimant who cannot produce his witnesses (§11) is the liar, and heirs may still sue a dead seller’s estate fivefold (§12–13). Then the household: stealing a man’s young son (§14), abducting or harboring palace slaves (§15–16), the runaway caught in the open — returned for two shekels (§17), interrogated (§18), kept (§19), or lost on the road under oath (§20). §21–25 are the burglar hanged in the hole he cut, the robber, the city that must restore what its district could not protect, and the firefighter who loots the blaze — thrown into that very fire.

sentences.l: If the-owner of-the-lost-thing witnesses knowing his-lost-thing not brought, a-liar (he is): slander he-stirred-up — he-shall-be-killed.
sentences.i: If the owner of the lost property has not brought witnesses who know his property, he is a liar who has stirred up a false charge — he shall be killed.

morph.gloss: if — the word that opens every law of the code
owner, lord: bēl ḫulqim “owner of the lost property”
the lost thing, lost property; ḫuluqšu “his lost property”
witness; pl. šībū/šībī
one who knows: šībī mūdi ḫulqišu “witnesses who know his lost property”; mūdûssunu “what they know”
the lost thing, lost property; ḫuluqšu “his lost property”
his / him
not (in subordinate and nominal contexts)
to bring, carry: itbalam “he brought”, lū ublam “let me bring”
liar; false: sar “he is a liar”
slander, malicious talk: tuššamma idki “he has stirred up slander”
to raise, stir up: tuššamma idki “he stirred up slander”
to kill; N-stem iddâk “he shall be killed” — the code’s death sentence

sentences.n: The procedure of §9–10 closes on the third possibility: the “owner” who cannot prove ownership is himself the criminal. sar tuššamma idki — “a liar: he raised slander” — and the death he sought falls on him, as in §1.

sentences.l: If the-seller to fate has-gone, the-buyer from the-house of-the-seller the-claim of-case that five-fold shall-take.
sentences.i: If the seller has meanwhile gone to his fate, the buyer may take from the seller’s estate five times the claim of that case.

morph.gloss: if — the word that opens every law of the code
seller: nādin “the seller (who)”
to, for
fate; one’s natural death: ana šīmtim ittalak “he went to (his) fate” = he died
to go: illak “he shall go”
buyer, purchaser
in, from
house, estate

claim, disputed amount

to take: ileqqi “he shall take”

sentences.n: ana šīmtim alākum, “to go to one’s destiny,” is Akkadian’s gentlest word for dying — the same šīmtum the gods fixed in the prologue. Even death does not close the seller’s account: his estate pays fivefold.

sentences.n: Due process on stone: the court waits. The six months are written with the moon’s own month-sign ITI, the number 6, and the little KAM that turns a numeral into an ordinal.

sentences.i: If a man has stolen the young son of another man, he shall be killed.

sentences.n: Kidnapping, in five short lines. ṣeḫrum “small” marks the victim as a child; the law needs no more words than that.

sentences.l: If a-man either a-slave of-the-palace, or a-slave-woman of-the-palace, or a-slave of-a-commoner, or a-slave-woman of-a-commoner, out-the-city-gate has-taken, he-shall-be-killed.
sentences.i: If a man has taken a palace slave or slave-woman, or a commoner’s slave or slave-woman, out through the city gate, he shall be killed.

sentences.n: The city gate — KÁ.GAL, the “great gate” — is the legal edge of the world: past it, a slave is stolen property in flight. Six laws on runaway slaves begin here.

sentences.n: šisīt nāgirim — the herald crying the runaway’s description through the streets. Ignore the cry, and harboring becomes theft of a person.

sentences.i: If a man has caught a runaway slave or slave-woman in the open country and led him back to his owner, the owner shall pay him two shekels of silver.

sentences.n: The other side of the ledger: catching a runaway earns a bounty. Two shekels — about a workman’s wage for two months — written numeral + GÍN, the shekel-sign, + KÙ.BABBAR.

sentences.i: If that slave will not name his owner, the catcher shall bring him to the palace; his case shall be investigated, and they shall return him to his owner.

sentences.n: warkatam parāsum, “to sort out the background,” is the code’s standing phrase for an official inquiry — the palace as registry of persons.

sentences.i: If the slave escapes from the hands of his captor, the man shall swear an oath by the god to the slave’s owner, and he goes free.

sentences.n: nīš ilim zakārum — “to name the life of the god” — is the exculpatory oath. Where no evidence can exist, the god’s name settles it; a false oath trades this life for divine justice.

sentences.i: If a man has broken through the wall of a house, they shall kill him in front of that very breach and hang him in it.

sentences.n: Burglary in a mud-brick world: palāšum is literally to dig through the wall. The punishment is staged at the crime — the burglar’s body walls up the hole he made.

sentences.l: and the-city and the-governor in whose land and district the-robbery was-committed his-property lost shall-replace-for-him.
sentences.i: and the city and governor in whose territory the robbery was committed shall replace his lost property for him.

sentences.n: Collective liability: catch the robber, or the town itself pays. The rabiānum — the mayor — answers for the roads of his district. Modern insurance in eighteen lines, minus the premiums.

sentences.l: If a-life (was lost), the-city and the-governor one mana of-silver to his-people shall-weigh-out.
sentences.i: If a life was lost in the robbery, the city and governor shall weigh out one mina of silver to the victim’s kin.

people; the household: nišīšu “his household”

sentences.n: A mina — sixty shekels — for a life the town failed to protect, paid to the survivors. The nišū “people” here are the dead man’s own.

sentences.l: If in the-house of-a-man a-fire broke-out, (and) a-man who to extinguish went, to the-goods of-the-owner of-the-house his-eye lifted-and
sentences.i: If fire broke out in a man’s house, and a man who came to put it out lifted his eye to the householder’s goods

sentences.n: īnam našûm, “to raise the eye,” is the idiom for coveting — the firefighter eyeing the furniture.

sentences.l: and the-goods of-the-owner of-the-house took, man that into fire that shall-be-thrown.
sentences.i: and took them — that man shall be thrown into that very fire.

sentences.n: The block ends as it likes to: punishment mirroring crime, the looter fed to the blaze he was pretending to fight. Harper preserves one last carver’s slip here — il-te-di carved where il-te-qí was meant; the DI sign sits where KI should be, and the stone keeps its own errata. §26 opens the soldier’s laws — the next installment.

glossary.šumma: if — the word that opens every law of the code
glossary.bēlum: owner, lord: bēl ḫulqim “owner of the lost property”
glossary.ḫulqum: the lost thing, lost property; ḫuluqšu “his lost property”
glossary.mūdûm: one who knows: šībī mūdi ḫulqišu “witnesses who know his lost property”; mūdûssunu “what they know”
glossary.wabālum: to bring, carry: itbalam “he brought”, lū ublam “let me bring”
glossary.sarrum: liar; false: sar “he is a liar”
glossary.tuššum: slander, malicious talk: tuššamma idki “he has stirred up slander”
glossary.dekûm: to raise, stir up: tuššamma idki “he stirred up slander”
glossary.dâkum: to kill; N-stem iddâk “he shall be killed” — the code’s death sentence
glossary.šīmtum: fate; one’s natural death: ana šīmtim ittalak “he went to (his) fate” = he died
glossary.dīnum: case, lawsuit; judgment: dīn napištim “a case of life”
glossary.5-šu: fivefold: adi 5-šu “up to five times over”
glossary.awīlum: man; the free citizen, the code’s first legal class
glossary.qerbum: near, at hand: šībūšu lā qerbū “his witnesses are not at hand”
glossary.adannum: set term, deadline: adannam “a term (of six months)”
glossary.warḫum: month (written with ITI, the moon-month sign): warḫim “of the month”
glossary.6-kam: sixth (KAM marks the ordinal): warḫim šeššim “of the sixth month”
glossary.šakānum: to set, place: išakkanūšumma “they shall set for him (a term), and”
glossary.redûm: to lead, conduct; bring back: irdiam “he produced”, irtediaššu “he has led him back”, ireddišu “he shall conduct him”
glossary.našûm: to bear, carry: ittanašši “he shall bear (the penalty)”
glossary.mārum: son (DUMU) — here: a minor, not yet able to sell
glossary.ṣeḫrum: young; the child: mār awīlim ṣeḫram “the young son of a free man”
glossary.lū: or; either (lū … lū “whether … or”); with verb: “let …”
glossary.ekallum: palace (É.GAL “great house”) — the crown as owner
glossary.muškēnum: commoner, dependent freeman (MAŠ.EN.KAK) — the code’s second rank
glossary.abullum: city gate (KÁ.GAL “great door”): abullam uštēṣi “he has taken out through the gate”
glossary.waṣûm: to go out; ūṣiamma “he came forward”
glossary.ḫalāqum: to be lost, vanish: ḫalqu, ḫalqam
glossary.rakûm: to harbor, conceal (a fugitive slave): irtakima “he has harbored, and” — Harper’s reading; modern editions take the verb as raqûm “to hide”
glossary.šisītum: cry, proclamation: šisīt nāgirim “the herald’s cry”
glossary.nāgirum: the herald, town crier: šisīt nāgirim “the herald’s proclamation”
glossary.ṣērum: the open country, the steppe: ina ṣērim “in the open country”
glossary.2: two — two vertical wedges: 2 šiqil “two shekels”
glossary.šiqlum: shekel — the basic weight of silver, about 8.3 g (written GÍN): 2 šiqil kaspim
glossary.kaspum: silver — the code’s money, written KÙ.BABBAR “shining precious-metal”
glossary.nadānum: to give, pay: inaddin, iddinam, iddinušum; nadānim “to give”
glossary.zakārum: to name, declare: izzakar “he named”, izakkarma “he shall declare, and”
glossary.warkatum: the circumstances, the facts of the case: warkassu “his case” (warkat + šu, t+š → ss)
glossary.târum: to return: ul itârma “he shall never again …”
glossary.kalûm: to hold back, detain: iktalāšu “he has kept him back”
glossary.qātum: hand: ina qāt “from the hand of”
glossary.ṣābitānum: the captor — the one who seized the runaway: ṣābitānišu “to his captor”
glossary.nīšum: oath (literally “life”): nīš ilim “the oath by the god”
glossary.ilum: god; here: the temple as owner
glossary.wašārum: D-stem (w)uššurum “to release, set free”: ūtaššar “he shall be released”
glossary.palāšum: to break through, breach (a house wall): iplūš “he broke in”
glossary.pānum: front, face: ina pani “in front of”
glossary.pilšum: the breach, the burglar’s hole: ina pani pilšim “in front of the breach”
glossary.ḫalālum: to hang up: iḫallalūšu “they shall hang him” (in the breach he cut)
glossary.ḫubtum: robbery; the plunder: ḫubtam iḫbutma “committed a robbery”
glossary.ḫabātum: to rob: iḫbutma “he committed robbery, and”; N-stem iḫḫabtu “(what) was robbed”
glossary.ḫabtum: robbed: awīlum ḫabtum “the man who was robbed”
glossary.maḫar: before, in the presence of: maḫar ilim “before the god”
glossary.bârum: to establish, prove; D-stem: ubârma “he shall prove (it), and…”
glossary.ālum: city (URU): ālum u rabiānum “the city and the mayor”
glossary.rabiānum: the mayor, headman of a town
glossary.1: one — a single vertical wedge (DIŠ): 1 mana “one mina”
glossary.manûm: mina — weight of silver, 60 shekels (about 500 g): 1 mana kaspim
glossary.šaqālum: to weigh out (silver = to pay): išqulu
glossary.išātum: fire: išātum innapiḫma “fire broke out”; ana išātim “into the fire”
glossary.napāḫum: to kindle; N-stem: innapiḫma “(fire) broke out, and”
glossary.balûm: to go out (of fire); D-stem bullûm “to extinguish”: ana bullîm “to put (it) out”
glossary.numātum: goods, household property: numāt bēl bītim “the goods of the house’s owner”
glossary.īnum: eye: īnšu … iššīma “he lifted his eye (to it)” — he coveted it
glossary.nadûm: to cast, throw (a charge; a body into the river): iddi, nadû, iddû
